name: Pineta Valley from Bielsa
name_original: Ordesa y Monteperdido por el Valle de Pineta
description: Cycle 27.0 miles through the Pineta Valley in Ordesa and Monte Perdido National Park, featuring forests, mountain views, and Embalse de Pine
region_intro: Hiking around Tella-Sin offers access to the Aragonese Pyrenees, characterized by its mountainous terrain, deep canyons, and lush valleys. The region, situated at the eastern edge of the Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park, features altitudes ranging from 700 to 2,000 meters. Hikers can explore diverse landscapes including glacial cirques, river valleys, and areas rich in waterfalls. Best hiking trails around Tella-Sin The most popular hiking route is Revilla Viewpoints - circular route, a 2.7 miles (4.4 km) trail that takes 1 hour 36 minutes to complete, offering expansive vistas of the surrounding Pyrenean landscape. Another top favourite among local hikers is Tella Hermitages Route, a moderate 2.4 miles (3.9 km) path. This route leads through ancient chapels and a megalithic dolmen, providing both natural beauty and historical interest. Local hikers also love the Revilla Village – Ermita de San Lorenzo loop from Miradores de Revilla, a 9.6 miles (15.4 km) trail leading through varied mountainous terrain, often completed in about 5 hours 32 minutes. Hiking around Tella-Sin is defined by mountainous terrain, deep canyons, and glacial formations. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy walks to more challenging routes. The routes in Tella-Sin are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 300 reviews. More than 2000 hikers have used komoot to explore Tella-Sin's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Tella-Sin?
A: Tella-Sin offers a diverse network of over 15 hiking trails, catering to various skill levels. You'll find a mix of easy, moderate, and challenging routes to explore the Aragonese Pyrenees.

Q: Are there any easy, family-friendly hikes in Tella-Sin?
A: Yes, Tella-Sin has several easy routes suitable for families. A great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e942400653/revilla-viewpoints-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Revilla Viewpoints - circular route&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 4.4 km long and offers expansive vistas. Another easy choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/41549860" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tella Village – Hermitage of San Juan and San Pedro loop from Tella-Sin&lt;/a&gt;, a shorter 1.9 km walk.

Q: Can I find circular walking routes in Tella-Sin?
A: Absolutely. Many trails in Tella-Sin are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e942400653/revilla-viewpoints-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Revilla Viewpoints - circular route&lt;/a&gt; is a popular example, as is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e938207311/tella-hermitages-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tella Hermitages Route&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you past ancient chapels and a dolmen.

Q: What kind of natural attractions can I see along the hiking trails?
A: Tella-Sin is rich in natural beauty. You can encounter spectacular water features like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/322882" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Soaso Steps Waterfalls&lt;/a&gt; and the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/323986" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cola de Caballo Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features glacial formations such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2441602" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Soaso Cirque&lt;/a&gt; and the majestic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/151545" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monte Perdido&lt;/a&gt; peak.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks to explore in Tella-Sin?
A: Yes, Tella-Sin offers a blend of natural and cultural heritage. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e938207311/tella-hermitages-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tella Hermitages Route&lt;/a&gt; allows you to explore ancient chapels and a megalithic dolmen. The picturesque Tella village itself hosts the Museum of the Witch and the Museum of the Cavern Bear, offering insights into local legends and history.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Tella-Sin?
A: The best time to visit Tella-Sin for hiking is generally from late spring to early autumn (May to October), when the weather is mild and trails are most accessible. During winter, some higher altitude routes may be covered in snow, offering opportunities for ice climbing or snowshoeing for experienced hikers.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced trekkers?
A: For experienced hikers seeking a challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/3993548" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Revilla Village – Ermita de San Lorenzo loop from Miradores de Revilla&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 15.4 km trail that leads through varied mountainous terrain, often taking over 5 hours to complete. This route offers significant elevation changes and rewarding views.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Tella-Sin?
A: The trails in Tella-Sin are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.8 stars from over 300 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the stunning Pyrenean views, the diverse landscapes ranging from deep canyons to lush valleys, and the well-maintained paths that cater to various fitness levels.

Q: Are there opportunities for wild swimming or water activities near the trails?
A: Yes, the region's rivers like the Cinca, Cinqueta, and Barrosa contribute to its hydrological richness. The Pozas de Salinas de Sin are accessible and charming natural pools located near the Ordesa y Monte Perdido Natural Park, offering spots for wild swimming. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/15013157" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Waterfall – Yaga River Pools loop from La Sierra&lt;/a&gt; also features water elements.

Q: Is public transport available to access the trailheads in Tella-Sin?
A: While Tella-Sin is nestled in a mountainous region, public transport options can be limited. It's advisable to check local bus schedules or consider driving to trailheads, especially for routes that start outside the main villages. Parking information is often available at visitor centers.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the hiking trails in Tella-Sin?
A: Many trails in the Tella-Sin area are dog-friendly, but it's important to be aware of local regulations, especially when hiking within or near the Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park. Always keep your dog on a leash, clean up after them, and ensure they are well-behaved around wildlife and other hikers.

Q: Where can I find parking for the hiking trails in Tella-Sin?
A: Parking is generally available in or near the villages that serve as starting points for trails, such as Tella village or Revilla. Look for designated parking areas, especially near popular trailheads. It's always a good idea to arrive early during peak season to secure a spot.
tips: The Pineta Valley is one of the four valleys that make up the Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park and one of the most majestic in the Pyrenees. The Cinca River is born in it. Its landscape is a compendium of green grass, meltwater, bare stones and eternal snow.
This reservoir is especially photogenic in the fall.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to road cycle this route?
faqs.answer: This specific route, "Ordesa and Monteperdido through the Pineta Valley," is designed for racebikes and typically takes around 2 hours and 14 minutes to complete. This duration is for the 43.5 km distance with 737 meters of elevation gain.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as moderate. While some sections in the Pineta Valley are well-paved and suitable for a moderate fitness level, the region also features routes with significant elevation gains. Beginners should be prepared for mountainous terrain and a good level of fitness is recommended.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the route?
faqs.answer: You can expect spectacular scenery as you cycle through the Pineta Valley, which is a natural gateway to Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park. The route features dense coniferous and beech forests, high mountain meadows, and views of towering peaks like Monte Perdido. You'll also encounter the source of the Cinca River and its dramatic waterfall, which is over 100 meters high.

faqs.question: Where does the road cycling route start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: A convenient starting point for routes in the Pineta Valley, offering direct access to nature, is often near the Parador de Bielsa at the end of the valley. While specific parking details for this exact route aren't provided, the Parador is a known hub for outdoor activities in the area, suggesting parking facilities would be available nearby.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to road cycle in the Pineta Valley?
faqs.answer: The best time of year for road cycling in the Pineta Valley is generally during the warmer months, from late spring to early autumn. This avoids the snow and ice that characterize the region in winter, ensuring better road conditions and more pleasant temperatures for cycling. Always check local weather forecasts before you go.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the road cycling routes in Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park?
faqs.answer: Within Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park, regulations regarding dogs can be strict, especially on trails. While road cycling routes may have different rules than hiking paths, it's generally advisable to keep dogs on a leash and be aware that access might be restricted in certain protected areas to preserve wildlife. Always check the latest park regulations before bringing your dog.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park?
faqs.answer: Ordesa y Monte Perdido is a National Park, and while there isn't typically an entrance fee to access the park itself, certain areas or activities might require permits, especially during peak seasons or for specific routes. It's always best to check the official park website or visitor centers for the most up-to-date information on any required permits or regulations for road cycling within the park boundaries.

faqs.question: What are some notable landmarks or viewpoints along the route?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you'll pass through the stunning Pineta Valley, known for its dramatic landscapes. A significant landmark is the Embalse de Pineta (Pineta Reservoir), which offers beautiful views. The valley also provides glimpses of towering peaks like Monte Perdido and the impressive Cinca waterfall.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this road cycling route in the Pineta Valley intersects with or runs parallel to several other paths. You may encounter sections of the Sobrarbe. BTT. Ruta 1. Camino del Canal, Bielsa - Refugio de Ronatiza | Bielsa - Tella, and the Senda Pirenaica - E24. Other paths like Bielsa - Javierre - Parzán - Enlace and Senderos del Sobrarbe are also in the vicinity.

faqs.question: What should I pack for road cycling in the Pineta Valley?
faqs.answer: Given the mountainous terrain and potential for varying conditions, it's wise to pack layers of clothing, including a windbreaker or light rain jacket. Essential cycling gear like a helmet, gloves, and appropriate footwear are a must. Don't forget plenty of water, snacks, a basic repair kit, and sun protection. A camera is highly recommended for the spectacular views.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or restaurants along the Pineta Valley cycling route?
faqs.answer: While the route itself might not have numerous establishments directly on the road, the Parador de Bielsa, located at the end of the Pineta Valley, offers dining options. It's a good idea to plan your food and water stops, especially for longer rides, as amenities can be sparse in the more remote sections of the National Park.
